notes: 4 clues per country, ordered hardest/most obscure -> most obvious, matching the format of the Americas and Europe batches.

clues: It's home to the last surviving wonder of the ancient world, the Great Pyramid of Giza.
The Nile, the longest river in Africa, runs through it.
Its ancient civilization developed one of the earliest writing systems, hieroglyphics.
It sits in the far northeast corner of Africa.
It's the largest country in Africa by land area.
Most of its territory lies within the Sahara Desert.
It gained independence from France in 1962 after a long war.
It sits along the Mediterranean coast of North Africa.
It's home to Chefchaouen, a town famous for its blue-painted buildings.
It's separated from Europe by just 8 miles of water at its narrowest point.
Its markets, called souks, are famous for spices and leather goods.
It sits in the far northwest corner of Africa.
It's the smallest country in North Africa by area.
Ancient ruins of the city of Carthage sit near its capital.
It was the birthplace of the Arab Spring protests in 2010-2011.
It's the northernmost country in Africa.
It holds Africa's largest proven oil reserves.
Over 90% of its land is desert.
It has one of the shortest coastlines relative to its size in North Africa.
It sits between Egypt and Tunisia along the Mediterranean.
It's the most populous country in Africa.
It's home to Nollywood, one of the largest film industries in the world by output.
Its capital was purpose-built in the 1980s to be more centrally located than the former capital, Lagos.
It sits on the Gulf of Guinea in West Africa.
It's the only African country never colonized by a European power.
It uses its own calendar, currently several years behind the Gregorian calendar.
It's considered one of the earliest regions where coffee was discovered.
It's in the Horn of Africa in East Africa.
It's home to the Great Migration, when millions of wildebeest cross the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem.
Its capital is one of the only major cities in the world with a national park inside its limits.
It's a major hub for long-distance running talent.
It sits on the Indian Ocean coast of East Africa.
It has three official capital cities, splitting executive, legislative, and judicial functions.
It has 12 official languages, more than almost any other country.
It's home to Table Mountain, one of the oldest mountains on Earth.
It sits at the southern tip of the African continent.
It's home to Mount Kilimanjaro, the tallest mountain in Africa.
The island of Zanzibar, famous for spices, is part of its territory.
It's home to the Serengeti, famous for its annual animal migration.
It sits on the Indian Ocean coast of East Africa.
It's nicknamed 'the Pearl of Africa,' a phrase credited to Winston Churchill.
It's home to more than half of the world's remaining mountain gorillas.
It's the source of the White Nile, one of the two main tributaries of the Nile.
It's landlocked in East Africa, bordering Kenya.
It was the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence from colonial rule, in 1957.
It's one of the world's largest producers of cocoa.
It was formerly known as the Gold Coast.
It sits on the Gulf of Guinea in West Africa.
It's one of Africa's largest oil producers.
Portuguese is its official language.
It endured one of Africa's longest civil wars, lasting nearly three decades after independence.
It sits on the Atlantic coast of Southern Africa.
Its name comes from an Arab trader who once controlled an island off its coast.
It's one of the world's top producers of cashews.
Portuguese is its official language.
It sits on the Indian Ocean coast of Southeast Africa.
Its capital sits at the meeting point of the Blue Nile and White Nile.
It's home to more ancient pyramids than Egypt, though smaller in scale.
It was Africa's largest country by area until South Sudan split off in 2011.
It sits in Northeast Africa along the Red Sea.
It's the world's newest widely recognized country, formed in 2011.
It split off from Sudan after decades of civil war.
It's landlocked, sitting south of Sudan.
It's one of the least developed countries by infrastructure in the world.
It has the longest coastline of any mainland African country.
It's located on the Horn of Africa, the continent's easternmost point.
Its capital was once nicknamed 'the White Pearl of the Indian Ocean' for its beaches and architecture.
It's shaped like the number seven on the map.
It's home to the world's second-largest rainforest, after the Amazon.
It holds vast reserves of cobalt, a key material in batteries for phones and electric cars.
The Congo River, the deepest river in the world, runs through it.
It's the largest country in sub-Saharan Africa by area.
Its capital sits directly across the Congo River from another country's capital, Kinshasa, the only place in the world where two capitals face each other like this.
Its territory is heavily covered by rainforest.
It's smaller and less populous than its similarly named neighbor, the Democratic Republic of Congo.
It sits on the Atlantic coast of Central Africa.
It's nicknamed 'Africa in miniature' for its wide range of landscapes and cultures.
It has two official languages, English and French, reflecting its colonial history.
Its national football team's success helped make it one of the continent's most recognized sporting nations.
It sits on the Gulf of Guinea in Central Africa.
It's the world's largest producer of cocoa beans.
Its capital is home to a basilica modeled after St. Peter's in Rome but even larger.
Its name is French for 'Ivory Coast,' reflecting its colonial-era trade.
It sits on the Gulf of Guinea in West Africa.
It's the world's largest producer of cocoa beans.
Its capital is home to a basilica modeled after St. Peter's in Rome but even larger.
Its name reflects its colonial-era ivory trade.
It sits on the Gulf of Guinea in West Africa.
It's home to Timbuktu, a city once so remote and legendary it became a byword for the middle of nowhere.
Much of its northern territory lies within the Sahara Desert.
It was once part of a wealthy medieval empire whose ruler, Mansa Musa, is considered one of the richest people in history.
It's landlocked in West Africa.
It's one of the hottest countries in the world, with much of its land within the Sahara.
It's named after the Niger River, one of the major rivers of West Africa.
It's landlocked, bordering Nigeria to the south.
It's one of the least densely populated countries in the world.
It's named after Lake Chad, once one of Africa's largest lakes, now dramatically shrunken.
It's landlocked in north-central Africa.
Its northern reaches lie within the Sahara Desert.
It borders Libya to the north and Sudan to the east.
It completely surrounds another country, the Gambia, on three sides.
It's home to the Pink Lake, whose waters turn rosy from algae.
Its capital hosts the finish of a famous off-road motor race, the Dakar Rally, named for the city.
It's the westernmost country in mainland Africa.
It's home to Victoria Falls, one of the largest waterfalls in the world.
It's named after Great Zimbabwe, ancient stone ruins built without mortar.
It was formerly known as Rhodesia.
It's landlocked in Southern Africa.
It shares Victoria Falls with Zimbabwe across the Zambezi River.
It's one of the world's largest producers of copper.
It was formerly known as Northern Rhodesia.
It's landlocked in Southern Africa.
It's home to the Okavango Delta, a rare inland river delta that never reaches the sea.
It's one of the world's leading producers of diamonds.
Much of its territory is covered by the Kalahari Desert.
It's landlocked in Southern Africa.
It's home to some of the oldest and tallest sand dunes in the world, in the Namib Desert.
It was the last African country to gain independence from colonial rule, in 1990.
It's one of the least densely populated countries on Earth.
It sits along the Atlantic coast of Southern Africa.
It's the fourth-largest island in the world.
About 90% of its wildlife, including lemurs, exists nowhere else on Earth.
It split from mainland Africa roughly 160 million years ago.
It sits in the Indian Ocean off Africa's southeast coast.
It's home to the Eye of the Sahara, a massive circular rock formation visible from space.
Over 90% of its territory lies within the Sahara Desert.
It was the last country in the world to formally criminalize slavery, in 1981.
It sits on the Atlantic coast of Northwest Africa.
It's nicknamed 'the land of a thousand hills.'
Its capital is often ranked among the cleanest cities in Africa, thanks in part to a monthly nationwide community cleanup day.
It's one of the last strongholds of the endangered mountain gorilla.
It's landlocked in East-Central Africa.
Its name translates roughly to 'land of upright/honest people.'
It was formerly known as Upper Volta before renaming in 1984.

Its capital's name is often shortened to 'Ouaga' by locals.

It has two major cities functioning as capital: one official, one the seat of government.
It was formerly known as Dahomey.
It sits on the Gulf of Guinea in West Africa.
It's one of the least developed countries in the world despite rich diamond and gold deposits.

It's nicknamed 'the Warm Heart of Africa' for the friendliness of its people.
Lake Malawi, one of the largest and deepest lakes in Africa, covers much of its eastern border.
It's one of the most densely populated countries in Africa.
It's landlocked in Southeast Africa.
Over 80% of its land is covered by rainforest, one of the highest rates in the world.
Its capital's name means 'free town' in French, founded by freed and rescued slaves.
It has one of the highest GDP-per-capita figures in sub-Saharan Africa, largely from oil.
It sits on the Atlantic coast of Central Africa, straddling the equator.

It fought a decades-long war for independence from Ethiopia, achieved in 1993.
It sits along the Red Sea in the Horn of Africa.
It's one of the least visited countries in the world.
